questions.prompt: During intake, a client says they do not want work on their gluteal area. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Explain it is required for therapeutic results and proceed anyway.
choices.B: Respect the preference, document it, and adjust the plan.
choices.C: Ask every 5 minutes until they agree.
choices.D: End the session because treatment cannot be effective.

questions.answer: Respect the preference, document it, and adjust the plan.
questions.why: Clients have the right to refuse any area or technique. Documenting preferences supports consent and continuity of care.
questions.trap: A assumes clinical need overrides consent.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to diagnose their low back pain. What is the best response?

choices.A: Provide your best guess and recommend stretches.
choices.B: Explain you cannot diagnose and recommend appropriate medical evaluation if needed.
choices.C: Avoid answering and change the subject.
choices.D: Tell them to look up symptoms online.

questions.answer: Explain you cannot diagnose and recommend appropriate medical evaluation if needed.
questions.why: Diagnosis is outside a massage therapist’s scope; referral for diagnosis protects the client and maintains professional boundaries.
questions.trap: A crosses scope-of-practice even if well‑intended.

questions.prompt: A client arrives visibly intoxicated for their appointment. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.A: Proceed with lighter pressure to keep them comfortable.
choices.B: Reschedule and ensure they have a safe way to leave.
choices.C: Offer coffee and continue if they agree.
choices.D: Only do stretching to reduce liability.

questions.answer: Reschedule and ensure they have a safe way to leave.
questions.why: Intoxication compromises consent and increases risk. Rescheduling and ensuring safety is the ethical, professional choice.
questions.trap: A ignores impaired consent and safety concerns.

questions.prompt: While discussing payment, a client asks you to bill for a service you did not provide. What should you do?

choices.A: Agree if it helps them afford care.
choices.B: Decline and explain billing must be accurate.
choices.C: Change the service name slightly to help.
choices.D: Agree if they pay you extra in cash.

questions.answer: Decline and explain billing must be accurate.
questions.why: Accurate billing is an ethical and legal requirement; falsifying records is misconduct.
questions.trap: C is still fraudulent even if it feels minor.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to share their health information with their spouse. What is the best next step?

choices.A: Share because the spouse is family.
choices.B: Share only with the client’s explicit authorization.
choices.C: Share verbally but not in writing.
choices.D: Share if the spouse promises confidentiality.

questions.answer: Share only with the client’s explicit authorization.
questions.why: Client information is confidential; disclose only with explicit client authorization per policy and applicable rules.
questions.trap: A confuses relationship with permission.

questions.prompt: A client makes repeated sexual jokes during the session. What should you do first?

choices.A: Ignore it to avoid awkwardness.
choices.B: Set a clear boundary and state the behavior must stop to continue.
choices.C: Respond with humor to diffuse tension.
choices.D: Continue but shorten the session quietly.

questions.answer: Set a clear boundary and state the behavior must stop to continue.
questions.why: Clear, immediate boundary-setting protects safety and professionalism. If behavior continues, ending the session is appropriate.
questions.trap: A can enable escalation and undermines boundaries.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to work under the drape in a way that violates your draping standards. What is the best response?

choices.A: Comply because the client requested it.
choices.B: Explain proper draping and proceed only with clear consent and appropriate draping.
choices.C: Proceed only if you avoid skin contact.
choices.D: End the session without explanation.

questions.answer: Explain proper draping and proceed only with clear consent and appropriate draping.
questions.why: Appropriate draping and explicit consent are essential boundary safeguards; proceed only within professional standards.
questions.trap: A follows a request that creates boundary risk.

questions.prompt: A client wants to privately pay you to extend a session beyond the clinic schedule. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Accept if it is cash.
choices.B: Decline and follow clinic scheduling and billing policy.
choices.C: Accept but keep it secret.
choices.D: Accept and skip documentation.

questions.answer: Decline and follow clinic scheduling and billing policy.
questions.why: Side arrangements undermine policy, billing integrity, and professionalism.
questions.trap: A seems harmless but creates ethical/policy issues.

questions.prompt: A client posts a public review describing their condition and thanks you. You want to reply. What is most appropriate?

choices.A: Confirm their condition and thank them.
choices.B: Thank them in general terms without confirming they are a client or discussing health details.
choices.C: Ask them to message you privately with more details.
choices.D: Share the review with their full name on your site.

questions.answer: Thank them in general terms without confirming they are a client or discussing health details.
questions.why: Even if a client discloses publicly, the therapist should not confirm the client relationship or discuss health details in public.
questions.trap: A confirms protected details and the relationship.

questions.prompt: A client declines abdominal work on the intake form. What should the therapist do?

choices.A: Proceed anyway if it seems indicated.
choices.B: Respect the decision and document the preference.
choices.C: Ask again later until they agree.
choices.D: End the session because abdominal work is required.

questions.answer: Respect the decision and document the preference.
questions.why: Informed consent includes the right to refuse any area. Documenting preferences supports ongoing consent.
questions.trap: A violates consent even if well‑intended.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to become friends on social media to discuss appointments. What is the best response?

choices.A: Accept; it is convenient.
choices.B: Decline and offer professional communication channels.
choices.C: Accept but do not respond.
choices.D: Accept only if they leave a review.

questions.answer: Decline and offer professional communication channels.
questions.why: Social media connections can blur boundaries; use professional channels that protect privacy and professionalism.
questions.trap: A increases boundary risk and privacy issues.

questions.prompt: A client requests you remove a safety item required by facility policy. What is the best response?

choices.A: Comply if the client insists.
choices.B: Follow policy and explain you must comply with requirements.
choices.C: Comply only for part of the session.
choices.D: Comply if they sign a waiver.

questions.answer: Follow policy and explain you must comply with requirements.
questions.why: Professionalism includes adhering to facility safety policies and communicating boundaries clearly.
questions.trap: D doesn’t override policy or duty of care.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to share your coworker’s schedule and contact information. What should you do?

choices.A: Share it to be helpful.
choices.B: Direct the client to the front desk or official scheduling channel.
choices.C: Give the coworker’s personal phone number only.
choices.D: Share it if the client is a regular.

questions.answer: Direct the client to the front desk or official scheduling channel.
questions.why: Use official channels and respect colleagues’ privacy; do not disclose personal information without permission.
questions.trap: A seems helpful but violates privacy/protocol.

questions.prompt: A client requests a technique you are not trained to perform. What is the best next step?

choices.A: Try it carefully since it is similar to what you know.
choices.B: Decline, explain your limits, and offer an appropriate alternative or referral.
choices.C: Watch a quick tutorial and attempt it.
choices.D: Do it only if they sign a waiver.

questions.answer: Decline, explain your limits, and offer an appropriate alternative or referral.
questions.why: Ethical practice requires working within training and competence; offer safe alternatives or refer out.
questions.trap: D waivers do not replace competence.

questions.prompt: A client asks for your opinion about another therapist’s skills in the clinic. What is the best response?

choices.A: Give your honest ranking of therapists.
choices.B: Avoid criticizing colleagues; help the client clarify goals and preferences.
choices.C: Tell them the other therapist is unqualified.
choices.D: Recommend whoever gives referral bonuses.

questions.answer: Avoid criticizing colleagues; help the client clarify goals and preferences.
questions.why: Professionalism includes respecting colleagues; guide the client to choose based on their needs without disparagement.
questions.trap: A can become gossip and damages trust.

questions.prompt: A client wants you to discuss their session details with their partner waiting outside. The client did not authorize this. What should you do?

choices.A: Share general findings without details.
choices.B: Do not confirm anything; ask the client privately what they want disclosed.
choices.C: Share because the partner is concerned.
choices.D: Share if the partner promises confidentiality.

questions.answer: Do not confirm anything; ask the client privately what they want disclosed.
questions.why: You should not confirm the client relationship or share details without authorization; ask the client what, if anything, may be shared.
questions.trap: A still confirms the relationship and may disclose information.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to keep a note-free practice because they dislike paperwork. What is most appropriate?

choices.A: Agree and keep no records at all.
choices.B: Explain you document minimally for care and continuity while protecting confidentiality.
choices.C: Document every personal detail to protect yourself.
choices.D: Refuse to treat clients who dislike documentation.

questions.answer: Explain you document minimally for care and continuity while protecting confidentiality.
questions.why: Minimal, relevant documentation supports care and professionalism; reassure the client about confidentiality and relevance.
questions.trap: C over-documents and harms trust.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to guarantee confidentiality in all circumstances. What is the best response?

choices.A: Agree; confidentiality is absolute.
choices.B: Explain confidentiality is maintained except for limited safety/legal exceptions and authorized releases.
choices.C: Say confidentiality does not apply to massage therapy.
choices.D: Say confidentiality depends on how they pay.

questions.answer: Explain confidentiality is maintained except for limited safety/legal exceptions and authorized releases.
questions.why: Confidentiality is standard practice, but exceptions may exist for legal requirements and safety concerns.
questions.trap: A is absolute and inaccurate.

questions.prompt: A client requests that you ignore their written boundaries 'just this once.' What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Agree if they insist.
choices.B: Maintain the boundary, re-confirm consent, and proceed only within agreed limits.
choices.C: Proceed but do not document it.
choices.D: End the session immediately without discussion.

questions.answer: Maintain the boundary, re-confirm consent, and proceed only within agreed limits.
questions.why: Consent and boundaries must be honored consistently; re-confirm consent and proceed only within limits.
questions.trap: A prioritizes pressure over professionalism.

questions.prompt: A client offers you cash to 'skip the intake questions.' What should you do?

choices.A: Accept; intake is optional.
choices.B: Decline and complete required intake/consent before treatment.
choices.C: Accept and do a shorter session.
choices.D: Proceed and fill the form later from memory.

questions.answer: Decline and complete required intake/consent before treatment.
questions.why: Intake supports informed consent and safety; skipping it undermines standard of care.
questions.trap: D leads to incomplete/incorrect documentation.

questions.prompt: A client gives you an expensive gift and says they will be offended if you refuse. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Accept it to preserve rapport.
choices.B: Decline politely, explain professional boundary policies, and suggest a small alternative (card/feedback).
choices.C: Accept it but discount the next session to balance it.
choices.D: Accept it and record it as payment in their file.

questions.answer: Decline politely, explain professional boundary policies, and suggest a small alternative (card/feedback).
questions.why: Large gifts can create pressure and a dual-relationship dynamic. Clear boundaries maintain professionalism while preserving respect.
questions.trap: A prioritizes feelings over boundary integrity.

questions.prompt: A client asks to record video during the session to post online. What is the best next step?

choices.A: Allow it since the client requested it.
choices.B: Follow policy; discuss privacy/professionalism and decline unless your clinic has a compliant process.
choices.C: Allow it if your face is not visible.
choices.D: Allow it only if they agree not to show the draping.

questions.answer: Follow policy; discuss privacy/professionalism and decline unless your clinic has a compliant process.
questions.why: Recording/video introduces privacy and boundary risks; follow policy and only proceed with a clear, compliant process.
questions.trap: C still enables privacy violations and misrepresentation.

questions.prompt: A client discloses a trauma history and becomes tense when you approach a new area. What is the best next step?

choices.A: Continue so they become accustomed to touch.
choices.B: Pause, check in, re-confirm consent, and offer options (change area/pressure/stop).
choices.C: End the session immediately.
choices.D: Tell them they must relax for treatment to work.

questions.answer: Pause, check in, re-confirm consent, and offer options (change area/pressure/stop).
questions.why: Trauma‑informed care prioritizes client control and consent, using check‑ins and options to support safety.
questions.trap: A can retraumatize and ignores consent signals.

questions.prompt: You realize you accidentally discussed a client’s appointment details within earshot of others. What should you do next?

choices.A: Do nothing; it was unintentional.
choices.B: Follow your privacy incident procedure: inform appropriate leadership, document, and minimize further disclosure.
choices.C: Ask the client not to be upset.
choices.D: Post an apology about it online.

questions.answer: Follow your privacy incident procedure: inform appropriate leadership, document, and minimize further disclosure.
questions.why: Professional response includes following privacy procedures, documenting the incident, and taking steps to prevent recurrence.
questions.trap: A ignores accountability and prevention.

questions.prompt: A client asks for a discount in exchange for leaving a five-star review. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.A: Agree; marketing is important.
choices.B: Decline and keep reviews voluntary without incentives tied to content.
choices.C: Offer a discount if they promise honesty.
choices.D: Agree but only for returning clients.

questions.answer: Decline and keep reviews voluntary without incentives tied to content.
questions.why: Incentivizing specific review outcomes undermines ethics and professionalism; keep marketing honest and voluntary.
questions.trap: C still pressures and can bias feedback.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to text them from your personal number for scheduling. What is most appropriate?

choices.A: Use your personal number; it is fastest.
choices.B: Use approved professional scheduling/communication channels.
choices.C: Text from your personal number but delete messages afterward.
choices.D: Use personal number only for preferred clients.

questions.answer: Use approved professional scheduling/communication channels.
questions.why: Professional channels protect privacy, boundaries, and recordkeeping. Personal numbers blur boundaries.
questions.trap: C is still boundary risk and reduces accountability.

questions.prompt: A client repeatedly tries to extend the session by asking 'just one more thing' after time is up. What is the best response?

choices.A: Keep going until they are satisfied.
choices.B: Set a time boundary, end on schedule, and offer to book another appointment.
choices.C: End abruptly without explaining.
choices.D: Continue if they tip.

questions.answer: Set a time boundary, end on schedule, and offer to book another appointment.
questions.why: Time boundaries are part of professional practice. Offer scheduling options without creating financial pressure or resentment.
questions.trap: A undermines scheduling fairness and boundaries.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to provide a letter stating massage will cure their condition for work accommodations. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.A: Write it to help them.
choices.B: Decline to make cure claims; provide only factual information within scope (e.g., sessions attended) if appropriate.
choices.C: Write it but include 'not medical advice' as a disclaimer.
choices.D: Write it and ask them to fill in the diagnosis.

questions.answer: Decline to make cure claims; provide only factual information within scope (e.g., sessions attended) if appropriate.
questions.why: Making medical cure claims is outside scope and unethical. Provide only factual documentation within competence and policy.
questions.trap: C still implies unsupported claims.

questions.prompt: During intake, a client reveals they are under a court order that restricts contact with a person you know personally. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.A: Discuss the order details with your friend.
choices.B: Maintain confidentiality; manage any conflict of interest through policy and consider referral if needed.
choices.C: End the session immediately and tell the client why.
choices.D: Proceed and ask the client for legal documents.

questions.answer: Maintain confidentiality; manage any conflict of interest through policy and consider referral if needed.
questions.why: Maintain confidentiality and professional boundaries; address conflicts through policy and referral when objectivity may be affected.
questions.trap: A violates confidentiality.

questions.prompt: A client wants you to treat them at home and suggests you keep it 'off the books.' What is the best response?

choices.A: Agree; it is common in wellness work.
choices.B: Decline or follow legal/business requirements (documentation, taxes, policy) and maintain professional standards.
choices.C: Agree if they pay in cash only.
choices.D: Agree but avoid writing any notes.

questions.answer: Decline or follow legal/business requirements (documentation, taxes, policy) and maintain professional standards.
questions.why: Off-the-books arrangements create ethical/legal issues and undermine professional practice. Follow legal and policy requirements.
questions.trap: C is still unethical/illegal in many contexts.

questions.prompt: A client requests you date them and says they will stop coming if you refuse. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Agree to keep the client relationship.
choices.B: Decline and maintain boundaries; offer referral if continued care is desired without dual relationship.
choices.C: Ignore the request and proceed with treatment.
choices.D: Continue treating them and discuss dating later.

questions.answer: Decline and maintain boundaries; offer referral if continued care is desired without dual relationship.
questions.why: Dating clients creates a dual relationship and boundary violations. Maintain professional limits and consider referral.
questions.trap: D keeps the door open and blurs boundaries.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to share your intake forms so they can use them for their new business. What should you do?

choices.A: Share them freely; forms are public.
choices.B: Decline unless you have permission to share proprietary materials; provide general guidance instead.
choices.C: Share if they promise not to copy them exactly.
choices.D: Share only if they credit you online.

questions.answer: Decline unless you have permission to share proprietary materials; provide general guidance instead.
questions.why: Professional materials and clinic forms may be proprietary; sharing could violate policy. Provide general guidance rather than documents.
questions.trap: A ignores ownership/policy.

questions.prompt: A client reports a new symptom mid-session that could indicate a medical red flag. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.A: Finish the session and mention it at the end.
choices.B: Stop, assess safety, and recommend appropriate medical evaluation; document the change.
choices.C: Ignore it unless pain is severe.
choices.D: Provide a strong technique to 'break up' the issue.

questions.answer: Stop, assess safety, and recommend appropriate medical evaluation; document the change.
questions.why: Potential red flags prioritize safety; stop and refer appropriately while documenting the symptom change.
questions.trap: A delays action on a safety concern.

questions.prompt: A client asks to remove all draping because they are 'more comfortable that way.' What is the best response?

choices.A: Agree if the client insists.
choices.B: Follow professional draping standards; explain boundaries and proceed only with proper draping.
choices.C: Agree but avoid sensitive areas.
choices.D: Agree only if no one else is in the building.

questions.answer: Follow professional draping standards; explain boundaries and proceed only with proper draping.
questions.why: Professional draping standards protect client and therapist; proceed only within ethical and policy boundaries.
questions.trap: C still violates boundary safeguards.

questions.prompt: A client requests you prescribe supplements to support recovery. What is most appropriate?

choices.A: Recommend specific supplements and brands.
choices.B: Decline to prescribe; suggest they consult a qualified healthcare provider for supplementation guidance.
choices.C: Recommend supplements if you disclose you are not a doctor.
choices.D: Provide a list of supplements you personally use.

questions.answer: Decline to prescribe; suggest they consult a qualified healthcare provider for supplementation guidance.
questions.why: Prescribing supplements is outside scope; referral to qualified providers maintains ethical practice.
questions.trap: C disclaimers don’t change scope.

questions.prompt: A client tells you they are recording audio without asking. What is the best next step?

choices.A: Continue; it is their right.
choices.B: Pause and address it immediately, following clinic policy; obtain consent or stop recording before proceeding.
choices.C: Ignore it as long as no video is taken.
choices.D: Ask them to send you the recording later.

questions.answer: Pause and address it immediately, following clinic policy; obtain consent or stop recording before proceeding.
questions.why: Recording impacts privacy and boundaries; address immediately per policy and proceed only with clear agreement.
questions.trap: A ignores professional boundaries.

questions.prompt: A client requests a refund and threatens a negative review if you refuse. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Give a refund to avoid bad publicity.
choices.B: Follow refund policy consistently and communicate calmly; document the interaction.
choices.C: Argue to convince them you are right.
choices.D: Offer a discount only if they remove the threat.

questions.answer: Follow refund policy consistently and communicate calmly; document the interaction.
questions.why: Consistency and professionalism require following policy and documenting coercive interactions; avoid negotiating under threats.
questions.trap: A rewards coercion and undermines policy.

questions.prompt: A client asks for a copy of their records. What is the best response?

choices.A: Refuse; records belong to the therapist.
choices.B: Follow your records policy and provide access appropriately while protecting third-party information.
choices.C: Provide records only if they pay extra.
choices.D: Provide records only to family members.

questions.answer: Follow your records policy and provide access appropriately while protecting third-party information.
questions.why: Clients generally have rights to access their records under policy/law; provide appropriately and securely.
questions.trap: A is often incorrect and damages trust.

questions.prompt: A client insists you keep treating despite your feeling that your objectivity is compromised (strong personal connection). What is best?

choices.A: Proceed; you can stay objective.
choices.B: Discuss boundaries and consider referral to avoid dual-relationship risk.
choices.C: Treat them only if they agree to not talk personally.
choices.D: Continue but avoid documenting the relationship.

questions.answer: Discuss boundaries and consider referral to avoid dual-relationship risk.
questions.why: When objectivity or boundaries are at risk, referral is appropriate to protect client welfare and professional ethics.
questions.trap: A underestimates dual-relationship risk.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to use a technique that causes pain and says pain proves it is working. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Apply painful pressure to satisfy the client.
choices.B: Educate on safe discomfort vs harm; use clinical judgment and avoid causing injury.
choices.C: Proceed and document that the client requested pain.
choices.D: Refuse all deep pressure work forever.

questions.answer: Educate on safe discomfort vs harm; use clinical judgment and avoid causing injury.
questions.why: Client preference doesn’t justify harmful care; educate and apply safe, clinically appropriate pressure within standard of care.
questions.trap: C treats documentation as permission to harm.

questions.prompt: A client discloses suicidal thoughts and asks you not to tell anyone. What is the most appropriate action?

choices.A: Promise confidentiality and proceed.
choices.B: Explain limits of confidentiality and follow appropriate safety steps and referral resources per policy.
choices.C: Ignore it unless they describe a plan.
choices.D: Call the client’s employer immediately.

questions.answer: Explain limits of confidentiality and follow appropriate safety steps and referral resources per policy.
questions.why: Safety concerns can require action within confidentiality limits. Provide appropriate resources and escalation per policy and applicable requirements.
questions.trap: A assumes confidentiality is absolute and ignores safety duties.

questions.prompt: A client asks you to falsify documentation to support an insurance claim. What is the best response?

choices.A: Agree if they are struggling financially.
choices.B: Decline, explain documentation must be truthful, and document the request per policy if necessary.
choices.C: Agree but keep notes vague.
choices.D: Delete prior notes to avoid involvement.

questions.answer: Decline, explain documentation must be truthful, and document the request per policy if necessary.
questions.why: Truthful documentation is an ethical obligation; participation in fraud is misconduct and creates legal risk.
questions.trap: C is still fraudulent and unethical.

questions.prompt: You receive a subpoena requesting a client’s records. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Immediately send all records.
choices.B: Verify validity and scope, follow legal/policy guidance, and disclose only what is required.
choices.C: Destroy records to protect confidentiality.
choices.D: Refuse under all circumstances.

questions.answer: Verify validity and scope, follow legal/policy guidance, and disclose only what is required.
questions.why: Legal requests require careful compliance while minimizing disclosure; follow policy and disclose only what is required.
questions.trap: A may over-disclose without verification.

questions.prompt: A client insists you treat an area you believe is medically unsafe today and threatens to file a complaint if you refuse. What is best?

choices.A: Treat lightly to satisfy them.
choices.B: Refuse unsafe treatment, document the interaction, and recommend appropriate medical evaluation.
choices.C: Treat if they sign a waiver.
choices.D: Argue to prove you are right.

questions.answer: Refuse unsafe treatment, document the interaction, and recommend appropriate medical evaluation.
questions.why: Professional duty is safe care; documentation and referral protect client welfare and standards. Waivers don’t make unsafe care ethical.
questions.trap: C is a common trap—waivers don’t replace duty of care.

questions.prompt: A client is also your direct supervisor at work and requests special treatment and confidentiality beyond your normal policy. What is most appropriate?

choices.A: Provide special treatment to maintain your job security.
choices.B: Address the dual-relationship risk and offer referral options to maintain professional boundaries.
choices.C: Treat them but do not document sessions.
choices.D: Treat them only outside business hours.

questions.answer: Address the dual-relationship risk and offer referral options to maintain professional boundaries.
questions.why: Dual relationships can impair objectivity and create coercion. Referral may be appropriate to protect boundaries and client welfare.
questions.trap: A allows coercion to shape care.

questions.prompt: A minor arrives with a signed consent form, but the signature appears questionable. What is the best next step?

choices.A: Proceed; you have a form.
choices.B: Pause and verify authorization using your policy before treating (contact guardian/office).
choices.C: Proceed but avoid sensitive areas.
choices.D: Proceed only if the minor agrees verbally.

questions.answer: Pause and verify authorization using your policy before treating (contact guardian/office).
questions.why: Consent must be valid and verifiable. Verify authorization before treatment to ensure legal/ethical compliance.
questions.trap: A relies on paperwork without validation.

questions.prompt: A client requests you share information with their physician and provides verbal permission only. What should you do?

choices.A: Share because it helps care coordination.
choices.B: Obtain appropriate written authorization per policy before releasing information.
choices.C: Share only the diagnosis you suspect.
choices.D: Refuse all communication with physicians.

questions.answer: Obtain appropriate written authorization per policy before releasing information.
questions.why: Release of client information requires explicit authorization; written consent is standard for documentation and clarity.
questions.trap: A may violate privacy rules/policy.

questions.prompt: A client offers you equity in their business in exchange for ongoing free sessions. What is the most appropriate response?

choices.A: Accept; it is a fair trade.
choices.B: Decline due to conflict of interest and boundary concerns; keep business relationships separate from care.
choices.C: Accept but reduce pressure during sessions.
choices.D: Accept only if they sign a contract.

questions.answer: Decline due to conflict of interest and boundary concerns; keep business relationships separate from care.
questions.why: Business entanglements create conflicts of interest and dual relationships that can compromise care and professionalism.
questions.trap: D doesn’t remove the ethical conflict.

questions.prompt: A client makes a reportable allegation about abuse of a vulnerable person but then retracts it and begs you to forget it. What is the best action?

choices.A: Do nothing since they retracted it.
choices.B: Follow your legal/policy obligations for reporting and document appropriately.
choices.C: Confront the alleged abuser yourself.
choices.D: Post about it anonymously to get advice.

questions.answer: Follow your legal/policy obligations for reporting and document appropriately.
questions.why: Mandatory reporting obligations (where applicable) and safety policies require following established reporting procedures, not informal actions.
questions.trap: A ignores potential duty to act.

questions.prompt: A client’s partner demands access to the client’s records, claiming legal authority, but provides no documentation. What should you do?

choices.A: Release the records immediately.
choices.B: Decline until proper documentation and client authorization are verified; follow policy.
choices.C: Provide a verbal summary only.
choices.D: Release records if the partner is upset.

questions.answer: Decline until proper documentation and client authorization are verified; follow policy.
questions.why: Verify authority and authorization before disclosure; follow policy and minimize disclosure to protect confidentiality.
questions.trap: C still discloses without authorization.
